Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered even though I put in around 25 hours a week. I'd sit down with good intentions, read through materials, watch videos, but I never felt like I was really absorbing things the way I should. I'd bounce between different courses since I'm juggling ten of them, and I think that spread me too thin. I tried to organize by writing out schedules, but I rarely stuck to them because something always came up or I just felt overwhelmed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have weeks where I was pretty consistent, logging in daily, and then other weeks where I'd cram everything near deadlines. With limited resources at home, sometimes I had to study when I could get access to better internet or a quiet space, so that threw off any regular schedule I tried to maintain.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an assignment deadline or just knowing I had to check if there was something new posted. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges or points. Sometimes fear drove me too—I'd get scared I was missing something important, so I'd check even when I didn't really need to.
Q4: Yeah, for a while there the gamified elements made it almost habitual. I'd wake up and check my progress, see if I'd unlocked anything new. It became kind of like checking social media, honestly. But that habit faded when the exam pressure built up and I realized the points weren't translating into actual understanding of the material.
Q5: The video lectures were probably most useful because I could pause and rewind when things got confusing. I also liked the discussion forums because reading other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one struggling. The gamified tasks kept me coming back, even if they didn't always help me learn deeply—they at least kept me engaged with the platform.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional PDFs. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize, and those felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on. Some of the longer academic articles just sat there untouched because they felt overwhelming and I wasn't sure they'd be on the exam anyway.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or progress—like completing a task and getting points, or finishing a quiz and seeing my score right away. If something felt like it would directly help me on assignments or exams, I'd engage with it. But if it was vague or the connection to my grade wasn't clear, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford. With ten courses and limited time, I had to focus on what was required. The required stuff had this weight to it that made me anxious if I didn't complete it, while optional things just felt like nice-to-haves that I'd get to 'someday'—which usually meant never.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs to my phone and review them on the bus or when my internet was spotty. I also wrote out notes by hand because that helps me remember better than just reading on screen. Sometimes I'd study with classmates in person, going over material together.
Q10: There were many times I'd be going through my handwritten notes for hours, really trying to understand concepts, but the LMS would show nothing. Or I'd watch a video once online, then replay it in my head while working my part-time job, thinking through the problems. That mental processing doesn't leave any digital trace but it's still studying.
Q11: Yeah, that happened a lot. I'd watch a video lecture once to download it or take screenshots of key slides, then I'd study from those materials offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed in one session and then work with it independently afterward.
Q12: Around midterms and when multiple assignments piled up, I really struggled to stay engaged. I felt scared about falling behind, which paradoxically made it harder to focus. There were also times when personal stuff—money stress, family obligations—made it hard to concentrate even when I was logged in and trying to study.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about finances, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other obligations, and the course often lost. The fatigue made everything feel harder than it should have been.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the lecture playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about bills, thinking about other assignments, just mentally checked out. I'd technically 'watch' 200 minutes of video but retain maybe half of it because I wasn't truly present.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams because the material scared me. I knew the exam content was important, but the anxiety made me avoid it and focus on smaller tasks that felt more manageable—like discussion posts or gamified activities. It was easier to feel productive doing those than to face the stuff I was genuinely struggling with.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd be relatively calm until about 48 hours before something was due, then I'd go into overdrive—logging in constantly, frantically completing tasks. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was often the only thing that got me to actually submit work.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it motivated me to engage more with the next assignment. But when feedback was critical or I got a bad grade, I'd sometimes avoid the course for a few days because I felt defeated. The fear of more negative feedback made me hesitant to check in.
Q18: The course structure with its gamified elements kept me logging in regularly, which I appreciated. But the structure didn't really help me understand the deeper material—I was going through motions, collecting points, without truly grasping concepts. The weekly modules were clear, but the connection between activities and exam content wasn't always obvious to me.
Q19: The gamification made engagement easier in terms of frequency—I wanted those rewards. But the exam format made engagement harder because it required a different kind of understanding than what the daily activities prepared me for. There was a disconnect between what felt engaging and what actually mattered for my grade.
Q20: That was basically me. I was highly active—logging in constantly, watching videos, posting in discussions, doing gamified tasks—but my exam score was terrible. I think it happens when you're engaging with the platform without engaging with the actual learning. You can be busy without being effective, and the LMS can't tell the difference.
Q21: I've seen classmates who barely touch the LMS but ace exams because they're studying from textbooks or other resources. Some people just learn better offline or already have strong foundations. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything—someone could be a genius who just doesn't need the platform as much.
Q22: Course logs can show frequency and completion—how often you log in, what you click on, whether you submitted things. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you understood what you watched, if you were distracted, or if you were genuinely struggling despite trying hard. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to check boxes. It's when you're curious, when you think about course concepts outside of study time, when you genuinely want to learn rather than just pass. I wanted to be engaged that way, but fear and overwhelm often got in the way.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time—scared of failing, scared of not understanding, scared of wasting my limited resources on a degree I might not finish. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with handwritten notes or the anxiety that made me avoid the hardest material. It wouldn't show that I was trying, even when my exam score suggests otherwise.
